title: Recession risk up as prices fall
full_text:
The falling property market raises the risk of Australia sliding into a recession this year as a shrinking wealth effect crushes the confidence of households and businesses, economists warn.
The nation’s economic growth in the March quarter was already a subdued 0.3 per cent and the June quarter could be worse as Labor’s budget tax changes combined with higher interest rates hurt house prices, construction activity and mortgage applications.
After data released this week showed home values in almost every capital city fell during June, AMP has forecast a national top-to-bottom price fall of 7 per cent, with Sydney prices likely to drop about 11 per cent.
“When people are losing value on their homes, even if the homes are fully paid off, it does impact how they feel,” Ray White Group chief economist Nerida Conisbee said. “In periods of uncertainty we do see quite significant declines in transaction volumes, and that’s where economic growth could be severely impacted.
“When a house sells, it kicks off a chain of activity; for example, people buy more furniture and cars, and removalists are employed. People are not really sure how far prices will fall, so there is hesitancy from that perspective.
“No one wants to buy in a falling market.” Betashares Capital chief economist David Bassanese said the psychological impact of weaker house prices prompted people to cut back on their spending.
“Falling house prices hurt household wealth, and consumer spending is 50 per cent of the GDP,” he said. Australia’s current economic growth was barely positive “so there’s a reasonable chance of an outright recession in the June and September quarters”.
Mr Bassanese said the budget sparked negativity through its tougher tax rules for property investors and capital gains tax increases on non-housing assets too, and financial markets had switched from their pre-budget forecast of two more Reserve Bank interest rate rises this year to “50-50 for one more rise”.
“The RBA wants the economy to slow … but I don’t think they want an outright recession, but the budget increases that risk.” While a full recession – defined as two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth – is no certainty, much likelier is another per capita recession, where GDP per person falls because economic growth does not outpace Australia’s migration-fuelled population growth of 1.5 per cent.
AMP chief economist Shane Oliver said recent falls in the oil price reduced some recession risk “but now we’ve got this added risk of the housing downturn on the back of the rate hikes combined with the budget changes”.
Dr Oliver said housing represented about two-thirds of Australians’ overall wealth and a full recession, while not his base case, was possible this year.
“The aim of the government to virtually eliminate property tax concessions – even new property gets sold eventually – was to make housing more affordable over time for first-home buyers, and an implication of that is house price falls,” he said.
“Whether it’s an unintended consequence or an intended consequence, one path to get to improved housing affordability is lower house prices.
“Treasury is talking about a 2 per cent fall, I’ve heard others talking about a 5 per cent fall, we were initially in that camp but now it looks like it could be a little more than that.” Dr Oliver said investment markets could be fickle and it was logical for potential investors to hold back on buying as they waited for an improved entry price.
He said significant property downturns could be debilitating for an economy. “They can lead to a spiral where prices fall, people feel less well-off, which leads to less spending in the economy, which leads to further price falls.” KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne said a technical recession was “not our central case”.
“Our central case is for a slowing economy that by the end of the calendar year will have GDP growth of only about 1 per cent, but we don’t get two quarters of negative growth,” he said.
“However, because that growth is so anaemic, the average person on the street will feel like they’re in a recession.
“When jobs growth is slowing, consumption activity is declining, investment activity is falling and people are finding it difficult to change jobs and not getting the pay rises they want to get while at the same time their mortgage costs are rising and inflation is still a problem, it’s going to feel pretty shitty for people out there.” Dr Rynne said GDP per capita declined in nine of the past 13 quarters “so we are continuing to make ourselves individually poorer”.
REA Group chief economist Anne Flaherty said though the impact of lower prices was a problem only for people selling at a loss, “when you know the value of your home has declined that creates a feeling that you’re losing wealth, and it makes people feel poorer and less likely to want to spend”.
She said: “For those who may have bought in the last year, particularly in places like Victoria where prices are down year-on-year, that feeling of losing your wealth is probably stronger.” WEALTH P28-29
